title: Laying and connecting soaker hose for bed irrigation
subtitle: Installing soaker hose under mulch for low-pressure, low-evaporation watering of vegetable and fruit beds
excerpt: Soaker hose delivers water directly to the root zone through tiny pores along its length, eliminating the evaporation loss of overhead irrigation. Under a mulch layer, it loses almost no water to the surface. A 15-metre loop serves a standard 3 m x 2 m raised bed. This covers the layout, pressure requirements, connection to a timer, and winterisation.
sourceNotes: RHS irrigation guidance for vegetable and fruit gardens. WRAP water efficiency in horticulture guidance. Irrigation Association (UK) guidance on low-pressure drip and soaker systems. Garden Organic advice on water conservation in the kitchen garden.

content.text: Soaker hose is a porous rubber or polyethylene tube that seeps water slowly along its full length at low pressure. Applied at 0.5-1.0 bar (the typical output of a water butt raised 1 metre), it wets the soil to a radius of 150-200 mm on either side of the hose. Spacing runs 300-400 mm apart covers a standard bed without leaving dry zones.

Flat soaker tape (drip tape): thin-walled polyethylene with evenly spaced drip emitters. Flow rate is very consistent along the run. Maximum run length 30-50 m before pressure loss affects end emitters. Suitable for single-season use on annual vegetable beds.

Round soaker hose (rubber or recycled tyre): thicker-walled, seeps uniformly along the full length. More durable than flat tape; can be left in place and reused for several seasons. Maximum effective run length 15-20 m. Suitable for permanent beds.
Measure the bed and calculate the hose length needed. For a 3 m x 2 m bed with 300 mm row spacing: 7 runs x 3 m = 21 m plus connection runs. A 25 m coil covers this with margin.

Lay hose in parallel rows along the length of the bed, spacing runs 300 mm apart. Loop the end of each run and connect back with a U-bend connector, or use a manifold at the entry end to run each row independently.
Cap the far end of each run (most soaker hose kits include end caps). Uncapped ends lose pressure and flood the end of the row.
Connect to a header pipe running along the edge of the bed. The header connects to the water source via a timer unit.

Cover with 50-75 mm of mulch (straw, wood chips, or compost). The mulch retains moisture from the hose and prevents surface evaporation. Leave the hose just below the surface; burying deeper than 50 mm reduces the lateral spread.
Mains pressure is typically 3-5 bar in a UK house. This is too high for most soaker hose (which is rated at 0.5-1.5 bar). Fit a pressure reducer at the tap connection.
A gravity feed from a water butt raised 1 m provides approximately 0.1 bar, which is slightly low for some soaker hose; a 2 m raised butt (0.2 bar) gives better flow.

Timer setting: run for 30-60 minutes at a time, 2-3 times per week in dry weather. The soil should be moist to a depth of 200 mm after each session; check by pressing a finger in near the edge of the bed, away from the hose line.

Disconnect and drain soaker hose before the first frost. Rubber soaker hose tolerates frost reasonably well when drained; drip tape does not survive freezing if water is trapped inside. For permanent beds, leave the rubber hose in place over winter but disconnect the timer and drain the header. Flat drip tape should be removed, rinsed, and stored coiled indoors.

attrs.symptom: One side of the bed is wetter than the other
attrs.cause: Pressure loss over a long run is causing lower output at the far end
attrs.fix: Shorten runs to 15 m maximum, or use a manifold that feeds both ends of the hose runs in parallel to equalise pressure.

attrs.symptom: Hose is seeping in localised jets rather than uniform oozing
attrs.cause: Debris has blocked the pores in most sections, leaving only a few unblocked points discharging at pressure
attrs.fix: Remove the end caps and flush the system at full mains pressure for 1-2 minutes to clear blockages. Fit a filter at the header connection to prevent future blockages.
